title: Bafta Awards and a Shouted Word
summary: At the Bafta film awards in February, a man named John Davidson, who has Tourette's syndrome, shouted a bad word that hurt people. This word was a racial slur, which is a mean word about someone's race. Two black actors, Michael B Jordan and Delroy Lindo, were on stage when it happened. Bafta, the group that runs the awards, said they did not do enough to protect guests, members, and viewers. They apologized to the black community, disabled community, and everyone watching. An independent review found that Bafta's planning was weak, but there was no bad intent. Bafta is now working to make things better so this doesn't happen again. The BBC also said the broadcast should not have aired the word.

What did Bafta say about their duty of care?

background_read: Bafta is an organization in the UK that gives awards for movies and TV shows. The Bafta awards are a big event where actors and filmmakers celebrate their work. Sometimes, live events like this can have unexpected problems. In this case, a person with Tourette's syndrome, which causes involuntary shouts, said a hurtful word. Racial slurs are words that have been used to discriminate against people based on their race, and they can cause a lot of pain. Bafta apologized because they want to make sure everyone feels safe and respected at their events.
Article_Structure: WHO: John Davidson, a Tourette's campaigner, and Bafta, the awards organization.
WHAT: A racial slur was shouted at the Bafta awards, and Bafta apologized for not protecting people.
WHERE: At the Bafta film awards ceremony, which was broadcast live.
WHY: Bafta's planning was weak, and they are working to improve so this doesn't happen again.

perspectives.description: Bafta says they failed in their duty of care but had no bad intent and are apologizing and improving.
